# 📝 Notes

Jun 3, 2026

## 8am AI\!

Invited [David Olsson](mailto:david@atomic47.co) [eric@scroll.ca](mailto:eric@scroll.ca) [giroday@gmail.com](mailto:giroday@gmail.com) [hanif.janmohamed@gmail.com](mailto:hanif.janmohamed@gmail.com) [Fulvio Ciano](mailto:fulvio@atomic47.co) [scott@driftresourcetechnologies.com](mailto:scott@driftresourcetechnologies.com) [Jason Kryski](mailto:jason@mttr.ca) [Jen Boger](mailto:jen@atomic47.co) [Shabraj Shahid](mailto:shabraj@mttr.ca) [spencer@ratiomachina.com](mailto:spencer@ratiomachina.com) [graham@ratiomachina.com](mailto:graham@ratiomachina.com) [Bailey Klinger](mailto:bwklinger@gmail.com) [andre.bertram3@gmail.com](mailto:andre.bertram3@gmail.com) [Spencer MacBeth](mailto:spencer@mttr.ca) [mike@smartdeskcrm.com](mailto:mike@smartdeskcrm.com)

Attachments [8am AI\!](https://calendar.google.com/calendar/event?eid=aW52N2w4MWJvNjJqYm5zYmQxZTMzYjllNmZfMjAyNjA2MDNUMTUwMDAwWiBkYXZpZEBhdG9taWM0Ny5jbw)

### Summary

The group analyzed Artificial Intelligence impacts on hiring and workflows and decided on architectural strategies for project management.

**Artificial Intelligence Workforce Shifts**  
Hiring credibility faces challenges as applicants use tools to bypass traditional screening processes. Participants prioritized architectural systems thinking over atomic task completion to improve professional work quality.

**Implementing Intelligent Project Systems**  
Centralizing fragmented data through automated dashboards fosters better project management and team cohesion. The group decided to adopt local models for persistent context management to ensure efficient, secure workflow integration.

**Automating Academic Teaching Tasks**  
Deploying local agent models effectively handles high-volume administrative requests while maintaining strict data privacy. These systems free up valuable resources for high-level research and teaching activities within academic settings.

*Rate this Summary:* [Helpful](https://google.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_4YkxrBAaiTVqYCi?isGoogler=false&isHelpful=true) or [Not Helpful](https://google.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_4YkxrBAaiTVqYCi?isGoogler=false&isHelpful=false)

### Decisions

## ALIGNED

* **Adoption of systemic work orchestration** The group aligned on the strategic principle of transitioning professional work practices from atomic task execution toward systemic orchestration and architectural thinking.

We've **updated the Decisions section** using your feedback.

Let us know what you think: [Helpful](https://google.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_5p6FWBVWvynleNU?isGoogler=no&isHelpful=yes) or [Not Helpful](https://google.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_5p6FWBVWvynleNU?isGoogler=no&isHelpful=no)

### Next steps

- [ ] \[Ying\] Draft Requirements: Define specific needs and concerns for the local Artificial Intelligence model implementation. Structure these as formal requirements to evaluate available options effectively.

- [ ] \[David Olsson\] Share Article: Provide the recommended reading materials on Artificial Intelligence model research and context management to support the project evaluation.

- [ ] \[Ying\] Gather Requirements: Define all specific use cases and automation requirements for teaching tasks. Document these details to guide the selection of appropriate system solutions.

- [ ] \[Ying\] Evaluate Automation Tool: Install and test the recommended local model agent software. Determine if the platform meets specific privacy and automation needs for routine email tasks.

- [ ] \[Ying\] Share Award Video: Email the recording of the teaching award ceremony to the meeting participants.

### Details

*Did the screenshots in this section make your notes [better](https://goo.gle/44IH7J9) or [worse](https://goo.gle/4b5ZWtJ)?*

* **Impact of Artificial Intelligence on Hiring and Credibility**: David Olsson and Ying discussed the challenge of verifying candidates in a job market saturated by Artificial Intelligence tools. They observed that hiring interviews and homework assignments are no longer reliable indicators of authentic capability, as applicants often use Large Language Models or avatars to present themselves, leading to a general loss of trust in digital interactions.

  ![][image1]

* **Internet Authenticity and Digital Signatures**: The group explored the erosion of trust regarding online content since 2023\. Ying noted that platforms are beginning to implement digital signatures for content generated by Artificial Intelligence, such as the initiative by Google and Alpha Company, to distinguish between synthetic and human-created information. They discussed the concern that in the future, the vast majority of online content will be generated by Artificial Intelligence, potentially creating "swarms" that could manipulate human personality and decision-making, such as in future elections.

* **Human Skills and the Humanities**: David Olsson and Ying reflected on the irony that as the world advances digitally, human-to-human connection becomes increasingly valuable. David Olsson remarked that the core of education and early development is largely about socialization and training people to be "good humans," suggesting that these foundational interpersonal skills are the most essential for future careers.

* **Communication Habits of Digital Natives**: Ying observed a disparity in how the younger generation communicates, noting that while they are proficient in expressing emotions via digital text, they often display a lack of emotional expression and engagement in person. David Olsson suggested that this might stem from a generation trained to interact primarily with devices rather than people, leading to a noticeable absence of emotional register in face-to-face interactions.

* **Challenges in Educational Engagement**: Ying and David Olsson discussed the struggle to maintain high levels of engagement in academic settings, noting that while some students show strong leadership and emotional intelligence, a significant portion remain passive. They contrasted modern educational experiences with those of previous decades, noting that high tuition costs should act as a motivator, yet a "factory mentality" remains prevalent, with limited human interaction in large lecture-based courses.

* **Economic Displacement and Spreadsheet Reliance**: Ying raised concerns regarding the tracking of jobs being replaced by Artificial Intelligence in California and the potential for increased social instability. David Olsson countered that despite technological advancements, a vast majority of business functions—including those at high levels—still rely on basic spreadsheets rather than complex software, indicating that widespread integration of advanced Artificial Intelligence is not yet universal.

* **Future of Knowledge Work**: David Olsson and Ying discussed how knowledge workers, particularly in software, are facing the first wave of disruption. They noted that while companies may focus on incremental improvements through better software, the promise of Artificial Intelligence is exponential; however, most organizations will likely struggle to realize this full potential because different parts of society will adapt at different speeds.

* **Government Regulation versus Technological Pace**: Ying noted the disconnect between the linear progression of government regulations and the exponential development of Artificial Intelligence, predicting that this gap will create future bottlenecks. David Olsson agreed that this shift is difficult, citing personal experiences with long-term acquaintances in the software industry who are struggling to find work as their specific skill sets become obsolete.

* **Struggles with Workflow and AI Productivity**: Hanif Janmohamed shared the difficulty of finding a "middle ground" when working with Artificial Intelligence, as traditional methods feel outdated, while new methods are hard to calibrate for consistent productivity. David Olsson and Juan agreed that while tools can increase speed and capacity, there is often rejection or a lack of understanding regarding how to properly integrate these new capabilities into existing workflows.

* **Shifting Focus to Problem-Solving**: Juan argued that the focus of technical work should shift from the specific programming language—such as React or Angular—to identifying the right problem and defining the best solution, as Large Language Models can handle the coding portion. They emphasized that success now relies on system-level thinking and ensuring the questions being asked are the correct ones, rather than simply focusing on task completion.

* **Function versus Architecture**: David Olsson and Juan discussed the difference between a function-oriented mindset and an architecture-oriented mindset, noting that many developers struggle because they are accustomed to performing "atomic" tasks rather than building and managing systems. They suggested that meetings should evolve from status updates to substantive discussions that resolve thematic issues, using Artificial Intelligence to fill knowledge gaps rather than just tracking individual tasks.

* **Incongruous Job Market and Hiring Hurdles**: Juan expressed frustration with the current job market, noting that despite many layoffs, it is difficult to hire people who understand how to navigate modern tools. Juan described the need to "hack" the interview process to distinguish between candidates who are simply repeating scripts and those who possess actual depth of experience, as many candidates are now using Artificial Intelligence to survive the screening phase.

* **Consolidation of Responsibility and Knowledge Ownership**: David Olsson described a trend where roles are consolidating, with individuals like product owners becoming increasingly valuable by synthesizing knowledge across architecture, infrastructure, and security. David Olsson introduced the concept of "Project State," a dashboard-based application designed to manage project information, lessons learned, and milestones, aiming to turn project management into an automated, integrated process.

  ![][image2]

* **Team Incompatibility and Workflow Resistance**: Fulvio Ciano and Juan discussed how the acceleration provided by Artificial Intelligence is often lost when teams do not use the tools in a compatible way. They noted that resistance often stems from stubbornness or a refusal to update old methodologies, which prevents teams from transitioning from being individual "doers" to collective "orchestrators".

* **Moving Away from Atomic Work**: Fulvio Ciano emphasized that the transition to working with Artificial Intelligence requires shifting away from small, atomic job pieces toward working at higher levels of abstraction and architecture. Juan and Fulvio Ciano agreed that since Large Language Models excel at producing atomic code, human developers should focus on providing the correct decision matrices and systems thinking to guide the models toward desired outcomes.

* **Difficulty of Granular Design**: Hanif Janmohamed shared the challenge of maintaining the middle ground in the design process, finding it easy to be abstract or focus on the "weeds," but difficult to stay in the middle. David Olsson acknowledged that this is a common experience because working with these tools in isolated sessions requires the user to manually carry context and familiarity from one task to the next, which is cognitively demanding.

* **The Project State Solution**: David Olsson explained that Project State is designed to solve the problem of fragmented information by aggregating data from various channels into a central, searchable substrate. This system acts as a memory and automates actions based on history, helping users avoid getting lost in the middle of complex projects.

  ![][image3]

* **Local Models and Context Management**: Ying requested guidance on training a local model to serve as a teaching assistant, specifically to remember and improve based on past tasks. Fulvio Ciano clarified that "training" is technically different from providing "context," and that maintaining a continuous connection between the model and the user's specific context is the key to achieving the desired performance without needing to retrain the model.

* **Recommended Tools for Contextual Knowledge**: Fulvio Ciano suggested using Obsidian in conjunction with a local model to create a personal wiki or knowledge base. This approach allows the model to reference past context as needed, which helps the user without requiring them to manually drag in information every time a question is asked.

* **Defining Requirements for Implementation**: David Olsson advised Ying to first build a set of requirements for what they need to achieve before seeking a technical solution. He recommended using a Large Language Model to research different options, which would help Ying understand the costs and trade-offs involved in various approaches, such as building a system versus using existing commercial tools.

* **Automating Repetitive Teaching Tasks**: Ying detailed a desire to automate routine, high-volume teaching tasks, such as handling student emails and requests for templates, to free up time for high-value activities. Jason Kryski and David Olsson noted that this is a solvable problem that can be addressed by simple, existing patterns involving agents, memory, and either cloud-based or local models.

* **Simplicity in Implementation**: Jason Kryski advised Ying to keep the solution simple, suggesting the use of existing packaged solutions or local agent harnesses that can handle the requirements without needing deep technical expertise. He noted that tools exist which can run on a Mac Mini or similar hardware, making it possible to create an automated system that handles routine tasks effectively.

* **Privacy and Human-Centric Systems**: Ying expressed a strong preference for local models to ensure privacy, given the university context. Jason Kryski suggested specific local agent tools that wrap memory and agents into a single package, which would allow Ying to create a human-centric system capable of managing multiple roles, such as teacher, supervisor, and researcher, without relying on external cloud services.

* **Closing and Future Research**: David Olsson and the group concluded by encouraging Ying to explore the suggested tools and continue using Large Language Models to help articulate and research the specific requirements for their projects. David Olsson noted that while Large Language Models are fluent, the user must provide the meaningful content to ensure the outputs remain relevant and accurate, and Ying shared the news of a recent teaching award to close the meeting.

* **Provost Award of Teaching Excellence and Innovation**: Ying announced that they are the recipient of the Provost Award of Teaching Excellence and Innovation, which will be presented at the convocation this Friday. While this is Ying's fifth teaching award, they noted that it is the most significant one they have received to date. There were only three individuals in this category, and Ying is the only research faculty member among the winners, as the others are teaching faculty. Furthermore, Ying is the first recipient from their faculty to win this award in its 20-year history. Ying plans to share the video recorded at the event with the rest of the group.

* **Meeting Adjournment**: Following the announcement, David Olsson and Juan offered their congratulations to Ying, and the participants exchanged farewells to conclude the meeting.
